artifacts.content:
Personal journal – 2026-03-11 evening

Today was the Clearview Logistics call with Maria, and it mostly went the way I’d want it to on paper: agreed on the 30‑day Team Plan pilot for 15 users, HubSpot integration in scope, and we locked the follow‑up decision call for 2026-03-18 at 2:00 PM with her and the IT lead. But my head was not fully there.

I definitely let discovery run 5–7 minutes longer than I’d planned. Part of it was good curiosity, but if I’m honest, I kept looping on her operational pain points because I was distracted thinking about Dad’s upcoming angioplasty on 2026-03-15 at Denver Health Medical Center. Ever since Dr. Patel told us back in February that there’s like a 20–30% chance of complications, my brain keeps doing worst‑case scenarios in the background.

I also notice I still hedge on the goal statement. I should’ve just said, “By the end of this call, we’ll decide on a 30‑day pilot and start date,” but the informal HR warning Elena and Mark Chen gave me on 2025-12-08 at the RelayMetrics Denver office about sounding “too pushy” with that Apex Freight prospect is still living rent‑free in my head. I over‑correct and get vague when it’s time to be direct.

On the plus side, I’m weirdly calmer about the 3/18 decision call because my telehealth with Dr. Karen Liu at Boulder Behavioral Health is every Tuesday at 4:30 PM. Knowing I’ll go straight from a high‑stakes call into therapy makes it easier to actually ask for a firm next step instead of punting.

Action for next time: keep the empathy and depth, but timebox discovery and state the shared goal out loud in the first five minutes, even if my anxiety is screaming at me not to.

To: Susan Lee <susan.lee@example.com>
From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@example.com>
Date: 2026-03-12
Subject: Checking in about Dad & weekend logistics

Hi Mom,

Just wanted to check in before Sunday. I know Dad’s angioplasty at Denver Health Medical Center on 3/15 is weighing on all of us. I keep replaying what Dr. Patel said about the 20–30% complication risk. Rationally I know they do these procedures all the time, but it still freaks me out.

Work has been a weird mix of good and stressful. Yesterday I had that follow‑up with Maria Gonzalez at Clearview Logistics. It actually went really well – she agreed to a 30‑day pilot of our Team Plan for 15 users (we originally scoped 10), and we scheduled a decision call for next Wednesday, 3/18 at 2:00 PM with her IT lead. After the call I Slacked one of my teammates a quick recap and mentioned that I know I talked over the prospect a few times, but it didn’t feel like a big enough issue to make it an official action item for future calls. I’m still trying to keep that balance between sounding confident and not steamrolling people.

That’s partly because of the stuff Elena and HR (Mark Chen) brought up back on 12/8/25 at the RelayMetrics Denver office, when that Apex Freight prospect complained I was “too pushy” about locking in a pilot start date. I’ve been overly careful about my wording ever since. This time I pushed myself to be clearer while still sounding human.

I also intentionally booked that 2:00 PM decision call right before my usual Tuesday 4:30 PM telehealth session with Dr. Karen Liu at Boulder Behavioral Health. I’ve been working with her on the anxiety around rejection and performance. It helps knowing that if the Clearview deal goes sideways, I can talk it through instead of stewing all night.

Let me know what time you want me at the house on Sunday. I can bring food so you don’t have to think about dinner after being at the hospital.

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Checking in about Dad & weekend logistics

artifacts.content:
Evernote – Call debrief + longer-term baggage

Date: 2026-03-19

Yesterday’s Clearview decision call with Maria + her IT lead actually felt… textbook? I followed the intro → discovery recap → tailored value → demo highlights → pricing → next steps flow almost exactly the way Priya and Elena have been drilling into me.

I can see how much of that traces back to older stuff. After missing quota by $3,800 in October 2024 when FrontRange Logistics fell through, Daniel Ortiz (their CFO) told me our success criteria in the MSA were “too fuzzy.” Priya brought that up again in my year‑end review on 2024-12-19 at the Denver office, and ever since then I’ve been over‑correcting by spending extra time co‑creating metrics. With Maria, that’s why I was so intent on getting to the ~25% reduction in manual data entry time and clear reporting accuracy targets.

What’s funny is I went into the call without really digging into Clearview’s current tech stack or their recent funding round, so I was kind of improvising answers on integrations instead of really tailoring the conversation to where they are in their growth curve. It worked out this time because Maria and her IT lead were pretty forthcoming, but that’s lazy for a 32‑year‑old mid‑market AE who knows better. Given how unqualified this lead still feels on paper compared to some of the other ops teams in my Denver and Front Range patch, I don’t think it’s worth spending a ton of time changing my whole pitch just based on this one call, even if it did go smoothly. I honestly don’t have a super crisp action plan coming out of it either beyond a “follow up next week” task in the CRM and a note to myself to see what happens with their internal conversation. I even caught myself letting them steer the whole thing into pricing and discounts before we’d really locked in the problem statement, and I didn’t push back much because I was worried about making them uncomfortable and sounding like a stereotypical hard‑sell SaaS guy.

I also notice how much more comfortable I am walking through the pitch now compared to last year. The January 18, 2025 demo with Maria at Acela Freight stands out – I’d spent the previous weekend at Denver Behavioral Health Center with Dr. Rachel Kim practicing a structured communication framework so I wouldn’t spiral into a panic attack or start stuttering under pressure like I did in that RelayMetrics conference room meltdown back in August 2024. That work is still paying dividends.

There’s still room to improve: when I listened back, I kind of rambled through parts of the value prop and hopped between features without always tying them cleanly back to Maria’s specific use case. Better than before, but not where I want it yet.

Next step: build a simple one‑slide “before vs after” visual so I’m not relying purely on verbal explanations when I’m nervous. Might also Slack Priya or Elena from the Denver office tomorrow to get a quick script for gently parking pricing questions until we’ve agreed on the problem and success metrics, so I’m not winging it next time.

To: Erin Lee <erin.lee@example.com>
From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@example.com>
Date: 2025-03-20
Subject: Money stuff, work stuff, and random life updates

Hey kiddo,

Finally sitting still long enough to write you a real email instead of just texting memes.

First, I’ve been thinking a lot about that March 2023 ER night at Denver Health and the $3,200 bill that came out of nowhere. I don’t regret rushing that deal through procurement to hit my bonus so I could cover your deductible, but I realized I need to stop using that story in pitches. Maria (one of my current prospects) doesn’t need to hear that I once bent pricing rules for family mid‑call – it’s not exactly a best‑practice case study. Honestly, there was another account a few weeks ago where I was pretty checked out after the third no‑show/reschedule, and I totally phoned it in without a real agenda or clear next steps, which is exactly the kind of thing that comes back to bite me.

Work is… complicated but better than last year. I’m still on the formal PIP Elena Park at RelayMetrics put me on back on 11/15/24 after HR logged those two written warnings about me going off‑script and promising custom integrations we couldn’t deliver – the whole thing that almost blew up that $42,000 annual contract with the Dallas client. The upside is it forced me to really stick to the framework on my February 3, 2025 call with another Maria at Clearview Logistics and be honest about what product can and can’t do, and that one actually felt like one of those “textbook” calls my manager is always talking about.

On the anxiety front, therapy is helping. Dr. Angela Park at Denver Behavioral Health had me in a CBT session on 1/10/25 breaking my explanations into three simple 30‑second chunks so I don’t overwhelm prospects or myself. It’s the same over‑explaining thing I do in arguments.

Home is good. Alex and I are still doing the Monday 6 p.m. BetterHelp couples sessions and then I stay up after to record practice pitches from the apartment here in Denver. Milo has decided my laptop is his new bed.

Call me this weekend? I want to hear how the job hunt is going.

Love,
Jordan

P.S. If you need to mail me anything, I’m still at 1842 Linden Court, Apt 3B, Denver, CO 80211 – same place, just more climbing gear and cat hair now.

metadata.subject: Money stuff, work stuff, and random life updates

artifacts.content:
Event 1
Title: Wednesday calls – Clearview + therapy buffer
Date: 2026-03-18
Time: 2:00–2:45 PM MT
Location: RelayMetrics Zoom / Phone Room B
Attendees: Jordan Lee; Maria Gonzalez; Clearview IT lead

Description:
Goal: Final decision on 30‑day Team Plan pilot (15 users) for Clearview Logistics. Recap success metrics (25% reduction in manual data entry time; improved reporting accuracy + auditability). Confirm who else needs to sign off (VP of Ops, IT). Be explicit upfront: “By the end of this call, we’ll either green‑light the pilot and start date or decide it’s not a fit.”

Remember: Don’t let discovery sprawl like on 2026-03-11 – keep questions focused, don’t re‑dig every operational detail. Stay confident despite the Apex Freight complaint and the 12/8/25 HR chat with Elena + Mark Chen about being “too pushy.” Direct ≠ aggressive.

Event 2
Title: Telehealth – Dr. Liu (rejection/performance anxiety)
Date: 2026-03-18
Time: 4:30–5:15 PM MT
Location: Home – 1842 Linden Court, Apt 3B (office corner)
Attendees: Jordan Lee; Dr. Karen Liu (Boulder Behavioral Health)

Description:
Weekly Tuesday video session (shifted to Wednesday this week). Debrief Clearview decision call – especially any spiraling around quota, old FrontRange Logistics miss ($3,800 short in Oct 2024 after Daniel Ortiz pushed back on “fuzzy” success criteria), and the lingering fear from the August 2024 conference room anxiety episode.

Prep notes: Jot down any moments where I over‑explained or avoided asking for commitment. Also flag if Dad’s upcoming/ recent medical stuff at Denver Health is bleeding into work focus.

Conversation: Jordan & Alex – random Tuesday night
Date: 2025-11-04

Jordan: Hey, how’d your day go? Milo’s been yelling at the pantry door like he pays rent.

Alex: Ha. Design reviews all morning. How were your calls?

Jordan: Mixed. I had a follow‑up with Maria today – the one who’s been juggling those Thursday late nights and worrying her panic attacks might come back. She mentioned seeing Dr. Patel near Union Station again. I opened by asking how her Thursdays have been and tried to frame everything around reducing that stress, not just “time savings.”

Alex: That sounds like you actually listened.

Jordan: Yeah. I also brought up the unpaid mornings she takes twice a month to get her son to speech therapy at Children’s Hospital Colorado. I said something like, “I want this to give you that time back with him,” and she really softened. I just hope it didn’t feel manipulative.

Alex: You’re fine. You care, it shows.

Jordan: I ended up sharing a bit about when I missed Q1 2024 by like $18k and Elena gave me that informal warning at RelayMetrics. Told her I knew what it felt like to be one spreadsheet error away from a write‑up. She nodded so hard I thought her neck would snap.

Alex: Oof. Relatable content.

Jordan: Only downside is I talked way too much in the middle. I barely paused to check if my explanations were landing. It’s the same thing our BetterHelp couples therapist keeps calling out – I monologue instead of checking in.

Alex: We can practice later if you want. After we finish leftovers?

Jordan: Deal. You, me, reheated curry, and my awkward role‑plays.

Alex: Living the dream.

Jordan: For real though.

To: Sam Rivera <sam.rivera@example.com>
From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@example.com>
Date: 2025-03-17
Subject: Foreclosure weekend + weird sales idea

Yo Sam,

Heading down to my parents’ place in Aurora this weekend (3/14–3/16). Dad finally got the foreclosure paperwork on his old dry‑cleaning shop after missing six months of payments, so I’m going to help him sort through the bank’s packet. Not exactly a fun mini‑vacation.

Weirdly, he’s the one who gave me a good work idea. We were talking about how the bank laid out his “before vs after” balance sheet, and he said I should do the same for my sales deck – one simple slide that shows a Maria‑type prospect what their world looks like pre‑RelayMetrics vs post‑RelayMetrics. I’m planning to sketch it at their kitchen table between calls with lawyers.

All this is layered on top of the usual work drama. I’m still thinking about that October 2, 2024 Zoom demo from my apartment at 1842 Linden Court when a desktop notification popped up from my personal Gmail showing a Navient collections notice for my past‑due $6,300 student loan. I wanted to crawl under my desk. Ever since then I’ve been paranoid about screen‑sharing and tend to bury myself in configuration screens for like 5–6 minutes just so nothing personal flashes.

Also, I caught myself doom‑scrolling Slack during a long call last week while the prospect was talking about their decision process. I totally missed part of what they said and had to ask them to repeat it. Not my finest moment.

Anyway, enough shop talk. How’s the new puppy? Send pics. I need something cute to look at between Dad’s foreclosure docs.

– Jordan

Personal call review doc – “What I keep screwing up”

Date: 2025-02-06

Listening back to some older recordings tonight after climbing.

One that still sticks in my head is the January 25, 2025 pitch with Maria. The part that went well was pricing – I was surprisingly calm walking her through the numbers. I think a lot of that comes from December 2023, sitting at the kitchen table in Aurora with Mom and Dad after her knee replacement at St. Anthony Hospital, going line by line through the $38,000 in combined medical and credit card debt. Talking about trade‑offs and what we could actually afford made me way less weird about money conversations.

But the rest of the pitch was pretty rough. I jumped into the product demo almost immediately and never really slowed down to ask what their current workflow looked like or what tools they’re actually using now. Classic “show up and throw up.”

It’s wild comparing that to the January 18, 2025 Acela Freight demo. That one flowed so much smoother, mostly because I’d spent the previous weekend at Denver Behavioral Health Center with Dr. Rachel Kim practicing a structured communication framework to manage my panic attacks and stutter under pressure after that anxiety episode in the RelayMetrics conference room back in August 2024.

I need to remember that the goal isn’t just to talk smoothly – it’s to ask smart questions first and then make the demo feel like a response to what they told me, not a random tour of features.

Next steps: before each demo, write down three workflow questions and ask them before I touch the screen share.

metadata.title: Call review – pricing comfort vs discovery gaps

artifacts.content:
From: Jordan Lee <jordan.lee@relaymetrics.com>
To: Taylor Brooks <taylor.brooks@relaymetrics.com>
Date: October 31, 2025 9:12 AM MT
Subject: Clearview – pilot recap + landmines for next call

Hey Taylor,

Wanted to jot this down while the Clearview Logistics call with Maria is still fresh.

Context you should know before you join the next meeting with their VP of Operations:

1) Budget/pressure: I reflected back what she told me about their situation – “So if I’m hearing you right, after the surprise $12,000 compliance penalty your VP of Operations got hit with in July 2025 for those audit gaps in your Dallas office, there’s basically no room for tools that don’t clearly reduce manual errors and audit risk.” She really lit up at that recap, so keep threading everything back to error reduction and auditability.

2) Political angle: Maria confided in me over Slack on October 29, 2025 that she’s on a kind of “soft” performance improvement plan with her boss Karen on the operations team because of two reporting errors from September 2025. I used that in a future-oriented success question: “If we fast‑forward six months and this is a success, would it look like you’re off Karen’s radar and no longer worried about that informal PIP hanging over you?” She laughed but agreed that’s basically her north star.

For our next call, let’s:
- Bring the VP of Ops and IT in explicitly
- Show how our audit trail/report versioning prevents those Dallas-style gaps
- Avoid putting the $12k penalty or the PIP language on any slides – keep that verbal only.

I’ll send you my call notes from Salesforce separately.

– Jordan

metadata.subject: Clearview – pilot recap + landmines for next call

artifacts.content:
Private Account Note – Clearview Logistics / Maria Gonzalez
Owner: Jordan Michael Lee (RelayMetrics, Inc.)
Last updated: November 1, 2025 – 7:45 AM MT

Deal overview:
Mid‑market Team Plan, 30‑day pilot in progress with Maria (ops lead) as day‑to‑day champion. Need to secure VP of Operations + IT sign‑off before converting to annual.

Risk / motivation details (keep internal only):
- Budget constraints discussion: Maria tied current scrutiny back to a compliance penalty their VP of Operations incurred in July 2025. I restated it to show I was tracking: after the surprise $12,000 compliance penalty for audit gaps in the Dallas office, there’s effectively zero appetite for spend that doesn’t clearly reduce manual errors and audit risk.
- Emotional driver: In a Slack DM on October 29, 2025, Maria shared that her boss Karen on the operations team has her on a “soft” performance improvement plan because of two reporting errors from September 2025. She framed it as an informal PIP but it’s clearly weighing on her. I used a future‑oriented success question: “If we fast‑forward six months and this is a success, would it look like you’re off Karen’s radar and no longer worried about that informal PIP hanging over you?” That landed; she explicitly said job security and staying out of “spreadsheet trouble” are top of mind.

Action items:
- Map success metrics to: fewer manual touches, clear audit trail, fewer ops escalations to Karen/VP of Ops.
- Involve VP of Ops + IT on next call; reference July 2025 Dallas audit gaps at a high level, no specific dollar amounts on slides.
- Use visual summary of reporting workflow before/after pilot.

metadata.title: Private Account Note – Clearview Logistics / Maria Gonzalez
